hero.title: 🧠 Neuro Block 2
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

sections.hint: Focus on lesion localization, gaze pathways, brainstem syndromes, and basal ganglia circuits.

topics.content:
• PPRF (Pons): Horizontal gaze center → coordinates CN VI + CN III via MLF.
  • Frontal Eye Field (Area 8): Drives contralateral gaze.
  • MLF: Connects CN III & VI → lesion = INO (adduction failure + nystagmus).
  • INO: Convergence intact (NOT CN III palsy).
  • One-and-a-half: PPRF + MLF lesion → only one eye abducts.
  • Eight-and-a-half: + CN VII palsy → facial weakness.

• CN II → Pretectal → bilateral EW nuclei → CN III: Pupillary constriction.
  • Pretectal lesion: Loss of direct + consensual reflex.
  • CN III: Eye down & out + ptosis + dilated pupil.
  • Nucleus of Cajal: Vertical gaze holding.
  • Superior colliculus: Visual reflex + head/eye movement (tectospinal/tectobulbar).

• Medial (3 M's): Motor (corticospinal), Medial lemniscus, Motor CN (III, VI, XII).
  • Lateral: Spinothalamic, CN V sensory, Sympathetic (Horner), Cerebellum.
  • Key: Ipsilateral CN signs + contralateral body deficits.

• Medial Medullary (ASA): Contralateral paralysis + vibration loss + tongue deviation (toward lesion).
  • Wallenberg (PICA): Dysphagia + hoarseness + ipsi face pain + contra body pain + Horner.
  • Medial Pontine: CN VI palsy + contralateral hemiplegia.
  • Lateral Pontine (AICA): Facial paralysis + hearing loss.
  • Weber (Midbrain): CN III palsy + contralateral hemiplegia.
  • Benedict/Claude: CN III + tremor/ataxia (tegmentum).
  • Parinaud: Upward gaze palsy + light-near dissociation.

• PICA: Lateral medulla → Wallenberg.
  • ASA: Medial medulla.
  • AICA: Lateral pons → facial + auditory.
  • Basilar: Pons → locked-in.
  • PCA: Midbrain syndromes.

• Function: Coordination (NOT strength).
  • Vermis lesion: Truncal ataxia.
  • Hemisphere lesion: Ipsilateral limb ataxia.
  • Signs: Dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia.
  • Purkinje cells: Only output (inhibitory).

• CN VII + VIII: Facial paralysis + hearing loss.
  • Cause: Vestibular schwannoma.
  • Key clue: Facial + auditory symptoms together.

• Pathway: Dentate → Red nucleus → Inferior olive.
  • Lesion: Hypertrophy (unique).
  • Signs: Palatal myoclonus + ocular oscillations.

• Direct: Facilitates movement (D1 ↑).
  • Indirect: Inhibits movement (D2 ↓).
  • Dopamine: Promotes movement (↑ direct, ↓ indirect).
  • Thalamus (VA/VL): Output to cortex.

• Parkinson: Dopamine ↓ → TRAP (tremor, rigidity, akinesia, postural instability).
  • Huntington: AD, CAG repeat → chorea + dementia.
  • Wilson: Copper → KF rings + movement + liver.
  • Hemiballismus: STN lesion → flinging movements.
  • Sydenham: Post-strep chorea (kids).
  • Tourette: Motor + vocal tics.

• CN: V, VI, VII, VIII.
  • PPRF: Horizontal gaze.
  • Middle cerebellar peduncle: Largest → coordination.
  • Lesions: Facial + eye movement + motor signs.

• Always localize: Medial vs lateral + level (medulla, pons, midbrain).
  • Eye signs: INO → MLF, gaze palsy → PPRF.
  • Facial weakness: LMN = whole face, UMN = forehead spared.
  • CN combos: VII + VIII = CP angle.
  • Movement disorder: Think basal ganglia pathways.

neuro_batch1.q: A 77-year-old woman has a sudden headache and loses consciousness. The next day, after she had regained consciousness, she was aphasic, a bit somnolent, able to answer questions with “yes” or “no,” but was unable to construct even a short sentence. The woman seems to be fully aware of her situation. MRI revealed a lesion in the cerebral cortex. This lesion is most likely located in which of the following?
neuro_batch1.l: Lesion of Broca’s area (inferior frontal gyrus: pars opercularis & triangularis) causes expressive aphasia with preserved comprehension.
neuro_batch1.h: Aphasic but understands → expressive aphasia → Broca

neuro_batch1.q: Dysdiadochokinesia is an impairment of:
neuro_batch1.l: Dysdiadochokinesia is a cerebellar sign characterized by inability to perform rapid alternating movements.

neuro_batch1.q: The term lentiform nucleus refers to:
neuro_batch1.l: The lentiform nucleus = putamen + globus pallidus, forming part of the basal ganglia.

neuro_batch1.q: A 59-year-old right-handed male was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of occipital headaches of 3 weeks duration. Two weeks prior to admission, the patient had a sudden onset of diplopia on forward gaze and a sensation of dizziness. One day prior to admission, he noted a sudden onset of ptosis of the right eye. The patient has a history of hypertension of two years. His blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. He is alert, oriented, and cooperative. His pupils are equal and reactive to light. The uvula deviates to the left side and there is deficient elevation on the right side of the palate. The patient also has nystagmus on left lateral gaze and his right corneal reflex is diminished. His facial expressions are full and symmetric; however, he does not respond to pinprick sensations on the left side of his face and left side of his body. Ataxia and intention tremor are present on his right lower extremity. Position, vibration, and touch modalities are intact throughout. What do you think his diagnosis is based on the options below?
neuro_batch1.l: Lateral medullary (PICA) lesion causes ipsilateral facial pain loss, contralateral body pain loss, nucleus ambiguus deficits (dysphagia), and cerebellar signs.

neuro_batch1.q: Which of the following statements about the globus pallidus is not true?
a: It is located adjacent (lateral) to the internal capsule
It receives excitatory axons from the caudate and putamen
It is the major outflow nucleus of the corpus striatum
It sends inhibitory axons to the thalamus
neuro_batch1.l: The globus pallidus receives inhibitory (GABAergic), not excitatory, input from the striatum (caudate + putamen).

neuro_batch1.q: A 50-year-old woman was diagnosed with paralysis of her right arm and leg that later became spastic with increased deep reflexes and a positive Babinski sign. There were also movements on the left extremities. The muscles of the lips and cheeks were weak on the right side, but she could tightly close both eyes and wrinkle her forehead symmetrically. An internuclear ophthalmoplegia was observed in which the left eye would not adduct on attempted lateral gaze to the right although both eyes converged on near objects. There was loss of vibratory sense and discriminative touch from the right side of the body. When protruded, the tongue deviated toward the right. Based on these findings, what is the site of lesion?
neuro_batch1.l: Combination of corticospinal, medial lemniscus, cranial nerve (facial weakness), and MLF (INO) signs localizes the lesion to the pons.

neuro_batch1.q: Which of the following is not correctly paired?
a: Lentiform nucleus – Globus pallidus plus putamen
Corpus striatum – Caudate nucleus plus lentiform nucleus
Amygdaloid body – Claustrum plus amygdaloid nucleus
neuro_batch1.l: The amygdaloid body refers only to the amygdala, not the claustrum, which is a separate structure.

neuro_batch1.q: The prefrontal cortex (areas 9, 10, 11 and 12) is essential for:
neuro_batch1.l: The prefrontal cortex (especially dorsolateral) is responsible for executive functions like planning, judgment, and foresight.

neuro_batch1.q: Lesions of the superior parietal lobule interfere with the patient's ability to combine touch, pressure, and proprioceptive impulses, so that he is unable to appreciate texture, size, and form. This loss of integration of sensory impulses is called:
neuro_batch1.l: Damage to the parietal lobe causes astereognosis—an inability to recognize objects by touch despite intact sensation.
neuro_batch1.h: Cannot identify object by touch = astereognosis

neuro_batch1.q: Adam is a 60-year-old man who is right-handed and was told in the past by his doctor that he has an irregular heartbeat. One morning, he woke to find that his face drooped on the right side and that he could not move his right arm or right leg. When he tried to call an ambulance for help, he had a great deal of difficulty communicating with the operator. His speech was slurred, nonfluent, and missing some pronouns. When listened to, Adam’s heart rate was irregular. He detected an irregular heartbeat. It was very difficult to understand Adam’s speech because it was halting, with a tendency to repeat the same phrases over and over. He had difficulty repeating specific sentences given to him by the neurologist. He was able to follow commands such as “Touch your right ear with your left hand.” His mouth drooped on the right when he attempted to smile, but his forehead remained symmetric. He could not move his right arm at all, but he was able to wiggle his right leg a little bit. Which of the following language problems does Adam have?
neuro_batch1.l: Nonfluent, effortful speech with preserved comprehension indicates Broca’s (expressive) aphasia.

neuro_batch1.q: Cerebral dominance refers to:
a: That hemisphere which is more tactual
That hemisphere which controls left or right-handed writers
That hemisphere where speech and language is controlled
That hemisphere which receives the majority of corpus callosum fibers
neuro_batch1.l: The dominant hemisphere (usually left) is defined by control of language and speech functions.

neuro_batch1.q: A 64-year-old woman is admitted to the emergency room after she experienced dizziness. Several days later, a neurological examination reveals that the patient is unable to move her right eye medially. Which of the following is the likely locus of the lesion?
neuro_batch1.l: Failure of medial eye movement (adduction) suggests internuclear ophthalmoplegia due to MLF lesion in the dorsal pons.

neuro_batch1.q: A 77-year-old woman has a sudden headache and loses consciousness. The next day, after she had regained consciousness, she was aphasic, a bit somnolent, able to answer questions with “yes” or “no,” but was unable to construct even a short sentence. The woman seems to be fully aware of her situation. MRI revealed a lesion in the cerebral cortex. This lesion is most likely located in which of the following?
neuro_batch1.l: The inferior frontal gyrus (Broca’s area) is responsible for speech production—lesions cause expressive aphasia.

neuro_batch1.q: A 55-year-old man lost consciousness suddenly. Upon regaining consciousness, he complained of double vision and a tremor in his left arm. He was mentally intact and his speech was articulate and appropriate. The right eye had a dilated pupil and he had altered strabismus. On attempted lateral gaze to the left, the right eye would not cross the midline. Although vision was normal, the right pupil did not constrict in either direct or consensual pupillary light reflex. Hearing was normal, as were the rest of the cranial nerves from 5–12. Pain and temperature sensation from the body and face were normal bilaterally. There was diminished vibratory sense and muscle strength in the left arm, which also showed an intention tremor, dysmetria, and occasional involuntary movements. Which of the following syndrome could possibly present these signs and symptoms?
neuro_batch1.l: Midbrain lesion affecting CN III, red nucleus, and medial lemniscus causes oculomotor palsy with contralateral ataxia and tremor (Benedict’s syndrome).
neuro_batch1.h: CN III palsy + tremor = Benedict syndrome

neuro_batch1.q: A 69-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by local EMS. He is somewhat somnolent and complains of a headache. The examination reveals normal cranial nerve function but a profound right-sided weakness and loss of pain sensation and discriminative touch in his right upper and lower extremities. MRI reveals a hemorrhagic stroke in the internal capsule. Based on this man’s deficits, this lesion is most likely located in which of the following?
neuro_batch1.l: The posterior limb of the internal capsule carries corticospinal and sensory fibers.

neuro_batch1.q: A 59-year-old morbidly obese man is brought to the Emergency Department following an episode at his home. The history reveals that the man has uncontrolled diabetes and hypertension. The precipitating factor for this visit was the sudden onset of involuntary flailing movements of his right upper extremity, which occur intermittently without warning. MRI reveals a well-localized infarct in the diencephalon. This lesion is most likely located in which of the following nuclei of the diencephalon?
neuro_batch1.l: Lesion of the subthalamic nucleus causes hemiballismus.

neuro_batch1.q: The family of a 55-year-old female noticed that she was experiencing headaches, having difficulty completing mechanical tasks such as tying her right shoelace, and inability to voluntarily move her eyes to the right. After being hospitalized, an MRI (shown in the following picture) revealed the presence of a tumor. Which of the following regions was primarily affected by the tumor that best accounts for the observed deficits?
neuro_batch1.l: The precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex) controls voluntary movement of the contralateral body.

neuro_batch1.q: A lesion in the cerebellopontine angle produces?
a: None of the above is correct
neuro_batch1.l: CPA lesions affect CN VII and VIII.

neuro_batch1.q: A 52-year-old male is admitted to a local hospital after he first began complaining of headaches, which were then followed by a significant weakness in his right arm and leg, slurred speech, and lack of expression on the right side of the jaw. An MRI reveals the presence of a well-defined brain tumor (shown in the picture below). Which of the following structures or regions damaged by the tumor best accounts for the observed deficits?
neuro_batch1.l: The internal capsule contains major motor pathways.

neuro_batch1.q: After his wife had a stroke, a husband notices that his wife's hair is always tangled and uncombed on the left side of her head, although on the right her hair is straightened and orderly. The left side of her face is usually with food remaining there, but the right face is always clean. She has stopped putting her ring and watch on her left hand. When eating, she never uses her fork on the left side of her plate and, surprisingly, she often asks why she was not given a fork to eat with. This woman's stroke most likely damaged which area?
neuro_batch1.l: Damage to the right parietal lobe causes contralateral hemispatial neglect.
neuro_batch1.h: Neglect of left side = right parietal lobe

neuro_batch2.q: The Purkinje cells of the cerebellar cortex:
a: Provides the only output to the deep cerebellar nuclei
Receives inhibitory input from climbing fibers
Are excited by the axons of the basket cells
neuro_batch2.l: Purkinje cells are the sole output neurons of the cerebellar cortex and send inhibitory (GABAergic) signals to deep cerebellar nuclei.
neuro_batch2.h: Only output of cerebellar cortex → Purkinje cells

neuro_batch2.q: Lucy is a 55-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes for more than 15 years. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Her speech was slurred and unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. At the emergency room, she was found to follow commands well, and although her speech was very slurred, it was logical in organization. The lower two-thirds of her face pointed to the right side when asked to protrude her tongue. Her right arm and leg were severely but equally weak. The left side was normal. Where is the lesion most likely located?
neuro_batch2.l: Equal contralateral face, arm, and leg weakness with dysarthria indicates a lacunar stroke of the internal capsule due to small vessel disease from hypertension and diabetes.
neuro_batch2.h: Equal face + arm + leg weakness → internal capsule (pure motor stroke)

neuro_batch2.q: Which of the following nuclei in the thalamus projects to area 3, 1, 2 of the cortex?
neuro_batch2.l: Area 3, 1, 2 is the primary somatosensory cortex. The Ventral Posterolateral (VPL) nucleus relays sensory information (pain, temperature, touch, proprioception) from the body to this cortical area.

neuro_batch2.q: Heschl’s convolutions are located in which of the following areas?
neuro_batch2.l: Heschl’s gyrus (transverse temporal gyri) represents the primary auditory cortex, located in the superior temporal lobe (Brodmann areas 41 & 42).

neuro_batch2.q: A 55-year-old overweight man collapsed while loading a truck. Examination revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, and he had no vibratory sense on the left side of the body. These findings suggest:
a: A lesion in the left medial medulla
A lesion in the right medial pons
A lesion in the right medial medulla
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the left medial pons
neuro_batch2.l: Medial Medullary Syndrome (Dejerine syndrome) involves the pyramid (contralateral hemiparesis), medial lemniscus (contralateral vibration loss), and CN XII (ipsilateral tongue deviation). Tongue deviates toward the lesion side (Left).
neuro_batch2.h: Contralateral motor/sensory + Ipsilateral tongue = Medial Medulla

neuro_batch2.q: Symptoms of cerebellar disease include all of the following EXCEPT:
neuro_batch2.l: The cerebellum coordinates movement but does not initiate it. Lesions cause ataxia, intention tremors, and dysarthria, but NOT paralysis (which is a sign of UMN/LMN lesions).
neuro_batch2.h: Cerebellum = Coordination; Motor initiation = Cortex (Paralysis ≠ Cerebellum)

neuro_batch2.q: A 40-year-old male has difficulty producing a smile from the left side of his face. Muscles are flaccid and the eyelids are open. Where do the cell bodies of origin within the CNS lie?
neuro_batch2.l: This describes a Bell's palsy or Lower Motor Neuron facial nerve (CN VII) lesion. The facial nerve nucleus is located in the lower pons.
neuro_batch2.h: Facial nerve nucleus (CN VII) → Lower pons

a: Transmits visual information to the cerebral cortex
Is a visual motor reflex center
Relays auditory information to the cerebral cortex
neuro_batch2.l: The Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) of the thalamus is the primary relay station for visual information from the retina to the primary visual cortex (BA 17).

neuro_batch2.q: A 50-year-old woman has right-sided spastic paralysis, loss of vibratory sense on the right, and internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) where the left eye would not adduct. Her tongue deviated toward the right. Where is the site of lesion?
neuro_batch2.l: The presence of Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO) signifies a lesion of the Medial Longitudinal Fasciculus (MLF), which is a classic localizing sign for the pons.

neuro_batch2.q: A 62-year-old man presents with slow shuffling gait, stooped posture, masked facies, micrographia, and a resting pill-rolling tremor. What is the diagnosis?
neuro_batch2.l: Parkinson’s disease is characterized by the 'TRAP' mnemonic: Tremor (resting), Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, and Postural instability, along with masked facies and micrographia.
neuro_batch2.h: Pill-rolling tremor + Shuffling gait + Masked facies = Parkinson’s

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion in the right paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) would most likely result in:
a: Inability of both eyes to look to the right
Inability of the right eye to adduct
Inability of the left eye to abduct
neuro_batch3.l: The PPRF is the horizontal gaze center. A lesion causes ipsilateral gaze palsy, meaning neither eye can look toward the side of the lesion.
neuro_batch3.h: PPRF = horizontal gaze center → both eyes move together

neuro_batch3.q: Which structure must be activated to initiate voluntary gaze to the left?
neuro_batch3.l: Frontal eye fields control contralateral gaze. The right frontal eye field activates the left PPRF to initiate leftward gaze.
neuro_batch3.h: Frontal eye field → opposite direction gaze

neuro_batch3.q: A destructive lesion of the left frontal eye field (Area 8) would cause the eyes to deviate:
neuro_batch3.l: A destructive lesion of the frontal eye field causes the eyes to deviate toward the side of the lesion due to unopposed activity of the opposite side.
neuro_batch3.h: Destructive FEF lesion → eyes look toward lesion

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion of the right medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) would most likely produce which of the following findings when attempting left gaze?
a: Right eye fails to adduct and left eye shows nystagmus
Right eye deviates downward and outward
neuro_batch3.l: In internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), the eye on the side of the MLF lesion cannot adduct, and the contralateral abducting eye develops nystagmus.
neuro_batch3.h: INO = ipsilateral adduction failure + contralateral nystagmus

neuro_batch3.q: Which feature distinguishes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) from an oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy?
neuro_batch3.l: In INO, convergence is preserved because it uses a pathway independent of the MLF, unlike CN III palsy where convergence is impaired.

neuro_batch3.q: A young adult presents with bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia. What is the most likely underlying cause?
neuro_batch3.l: Bilateral INO is classically associated with multiple sclerosis, especially in younger patients.

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion affecting both the right PPRF and right MLF would most likely result in which of the following findings?
a: Neither eye can look right, and the right eye cannot adduct on left gaze
Right eye cannot abduct and left eye cannot adduct
neuro_batch3.l: One-and-a-half syndrome combines ipsilateral gaze palsy (PPRF lesion) and INO (MLF lesion). Thus, neither eye looks toward the lesion, and the ipsilateral eye cannot adduct when looking away.

neuro_batch3.q: In one-and-a-half syndrome, which eye movement is preserved?
neuro_batch3.l: The only preserved movement is abduction of the contralateral eye, as the ipsilateral PPRF and MLF are both damaged.
neuro_batch3.h: Only ONE eye moves ONE way

neuro_batch3.q: A patient with one-and-a-half syndrome attempts to look away from the lesion side. What is observed?
a: Ipsilateral eye fails to adduct, contralateral eye abducts with nystagmus
neuro_batch3.l: When looking away from the lesion, the ipsilateral MLF lesion prevents adduction, while the contralateral eye abducts and often shows nystagmus (INO component).

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion in the facial colliculus region of the pons results in one-and-a-half syndrome plus ipsilateral facial paralysis. What is this condition called?
neuro_batch3.l: Eight-and-a-half syndrome is defined as one-and-a-half syndrome plus ipsilateral CN VII palsy due to involvement of the facial nerve fibers looping around the abducens nucleus.

neuro_batch3.q: Which additional deficit distinguishes eight-and-a-half syndrome from one-and-a-half syndrome?
a: Ipsilateral lower motor neuron facial paralysis
neuro_batch3.l: Eight-and-a-half syndrome includes ipsilateral CN VII palsy, resulting in complete facial weakness on the same side.

neuro_batch3.q: A patient presents with inability to gaze toward the right, failure of the right eye to adduct on left gaze, and right-sided facial paralysis involving the forehead. Where is the lesion located?
neuro_batch3.l: This presentation is classic for eight-and-a-half syndrome, which localizes to the ipsilateral pons involving the PPRF, MLF, and CN VII fibers.

neuro_batch3.q: Shining light in one eye normally causes constriction of both pupils. This is due to projections from the pretectal nucleus to:
neuro_batch3.l: The pretectal nucleus sends bilateral projections to both Edinger-Westphal nuclei, producing both direct and consensual pupillary constriction.

neuro_batch3.q: Which structure serves as the afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex?
neuro_batch3.l: The afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex is carried by the optic nerve (CN II), which transmits light information to the pretectal nucleus.

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion of the pretectal nucleus would most likely result in which of the following findings when light is shined in one eye?
a: No direct or consensual pupillary constriction
Normal pupillary constriction in both eyes
neuro_batch3.l: The pretectal nucleus is essential for relaying signals to both Edinger-Westphal nuclei. Damage results in loss of both direct and consensual light reflexes.
neuro_batch3.h: Pretectal lesion → no light reflex at all
